coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional N-doped graphene quantum dots towards tetracycline detection, temperature sensing and high-performance WLEDs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yongsheng
Liu, Zhiduo
Chen, Da
Gu, Bingli
Gao, Bo
Wang, Zihao
Guo, Qinglei
Wang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high blue fluorescence are synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method from benzimidazoles. The as-fabricated N-GQDs possess a homogeneous size of 3.8 nm, good crystallinity, pip photostability, good water soluble, and a high quantum yield of about 25 %. In addition, it is found that the fluorescence of N-GQDs can be obviously quenched by tetracycline (TC) and elevated temperature. The detection of TC is implemented, with a detection limit of 0.21 μmol L−1. For the temperature sensing, the fluorescence intensity shows a linear reduction between 25 °C and 100 °C. Moreover, reversible and stable abilities of temperature sensing are demonstrated. Owing to the superior optoelectronic properties, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) using the N-GQDs as the phosphor are fabricated, and the CIE coordinates is (0.357, 0.315) with the color temperature of 4427 K. The results reported here will expand the multiple applications of N-GQDs in environment monitoring, biosensing engineering and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Tetracycline detection
Temperature sensing
Multifunctional nanosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: High-Quality white LED assembled by the N-GQDs phosphors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the obtained N-GQDs, the inset shows particle size distribution (top right) and HRTEM image of N-GQDs (bottom left). (b) AFM image of N-GQDs on a Si/SiO2 substrate. (c) Raman spectrum of N-GQDs on a Si/SiO2 substrate. (d) XRD spectrum of the N-GQDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of the prepared N-GQDs. The deconvolution spectrum of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (e) O 1s. (e) The FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. (f) Schematic diagram showing the structure of N-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis, PL and PLE spectra of N-GQDs. The insets show the images of N-GQDs solution under sunshine (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (b) The emission spectra of N-GQDs under different excitations from 315 to 405 nm with a 10 nm increment. (c) The two-dimensional mapping result of the emission (λem) varying with the excitation (λex). The normalized fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs under (d) various pH values, (e) ultraviolet light irradiation (150 W and 365 nm mercury lamp), and (f) different storage times.

(a) Influence of different antibiotics and amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs. (b) Images of N-GQDs in the presence of various concentrations of TC under the excitation of a UV light. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs at various concentrations of TC ranging from 0 to 400 μM. The excitation wavelength is fixed at 357 nm. (d) The relationship between (F0-F)/F and the concentration of TC, and the insert is the linear relationship between fluorescence and TC concentration ranging from 0 to 60 μM. F and F0 are the PL intensity of N-GQDs with and without TC, respectively.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-GQDs, TC and the mixture of the N-GQDs and TC. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TC, and the PLE and PL spectra of the N-GQDs. (c) The fluorescence decay profiles for the N-GQDs in the absence and presence of TC.

(a) The PL spectra of N-GQDs at different temperatures. (b) Infrared thermal and fluorescence images of N-GQDs solutions in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube at 10 and 80 °C. (c) Fluorescence intensity variations of N-GQDs under heating and cooling conditions. (d) The reversible temperature-dependent fluorescence of the N-GQDs.
(a) The emitting light spectrum of the as-prepared WLED. Inset is the optical image of a typical WLED device. (b) The CIE color coordinates of WLED.
Schematic of synthesis N-GQDs and the multifunctional applications.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional N-doped graphene quantum dots towards tetracycline detection, temperature sensing and high-performance WLEDs

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The as-fabricated N-GQDs possess a homogeneous size of 3.8 nm, good crystallinity, pip photostability, good water soluble, and a high quantum yield of about 25 %. In addition, it is found that the fluorescence of N-GQDs can be obviously quenched by tetracycline (TC) and elevated temperature. The detection limit of TC is 0.21 μmol L−1, and the fluorescence intensity shows a linear reduction between 25 °C and 100 °C. Owing to the superior optoelectronic properties, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) using the N-GQDs as the phosphor are fabricated, and the CIE coordinates is (0.357, 0.315) with the color temperature of 4427 K.

In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high blue fluorescence are synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method from benzimidazoles. The as-fabricated N-GQDs possess a homogeneous size of 3.8 nm, good crystallinity, pip photostability, good water soluble, and a high quantum yield of about 25 %. In addition, it is found that the fluorescence of N-GQDs can be obviously quenched by tetracycline (TC) and elevated temperature. The detection of TC is implemented, with a detection limit of 0.21 μmol L−1. For the temperature sensing, the fluorescence intensity shows a linear reduction between 25 °C and 100 °C. Moreover, reversible and stable abilities of temperature sensing are demonstrated. Owing to the superior optoelectronic properties, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) using the N-GQDs as the phosphor are fabricated, and the CIE coordinates is (0.357, 0.315) with the color temperature of 4427 K. The results reported here will expand the multiple applications of N-GQDs in environment monitoring, biosensing engineering and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel kind of quasi-spherical-dimensional and zero-dimensional carbon-based semiconductor nanomaterials with the size less than 10 nm [1–3]. In recent years, GQDs have emerged as a promising candidate for applications in fluorescence probes [4], light emitting devices [5], photocatalysis and sensors [6], because of their outstanding luminescence properties [7], attractive interfacial properties [8], high stabilities and distinctive biocompatibilities [9]. Until now, continuous efforts have been pursued to efficiently synthesize GQDs with superior crystallinity and highly fluorescent intensity [10]. In brief, the synthesized approach of GQDs can be classified into two strategies, i.e., the top-down and bottom-up technique [11,12]. The top-down method mainly involves exfoliation and cutting of large-scale carbon materials (such as block graphite and carbon fiber) by the process, such as chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation and laser ablation [13,14]. Although the prepared GQDs show uniform size and good crystallinity, the synthesizing process is complex and not environmental-friendly. By contrast, the bottom-up method is simpler and more efficient, which mainly contains dehydrogenation and carbonization of small molecule (for example citric acid, glucose, carbamide, grass, cotton, coffee ground, etc.) to form GQDs under microwave radiation, hydrothermal treatment and plasma treatment [15]. In particular, the hydrothermal treatment has been extensively used to prepare GQDs due to its simplicity, high efficiency, and environmental friendliness [16]. In addition, abundant research efforts have been focused on improving the fluorescence of GQDs, of which the heteroatom-doping is considered as a effective method to improve physicochemical properties, fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and long-term stability of GQDs [17,18]. Compared with pure GQDs, heteroatom-doped GQDs have wider potentials in optoelectronic devices, biological imaging, and fluorescence sensing [19,20]. Especially in optoelectronic devices and fluorescence sensing fields, the long-term stability and high QY of GQDs are required. For instance, Qi et al. developed N-GQDs via using rice residue and glycine as precursor materials, and the fluorescence could be sensitively quenched by Fe3+ ions and tetracycline [21]. Zhang et al. demonstrated N-GQDs with high luminance properties by one-step hydrothermal treatment as multifunctional sensor for the detection of temperature, Hg2+ and biothiols [22]. Geng et al. employed a multifunctional N-GQDs with the fluorescence quantum yield of 29 % for trace water detection, white LEDs and bioimaging [23]. Despite great efforts having been made, developing simply-synthesized, high stability GQDs with multi-functional applications is still a fascinating prospect.

Herein, we report a facile and gentle strategy to synthesize N-GQDs through utilizing benzimidazoles as precursor under the hydrothermal treatment, as schematically shown in Scheme 1 . The as-synthesized N-GQDs possess uniform size distribution, perfect stability and hydrophilic solubility. More importantly, the obtained N-GQDs exhibit highly selective and sensitive detection of TC, recognized by fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs reduces linearly with the addition of TC (the concentration varies from 0 to 60 μM), and the detection limit is evaluated to be 0.21 μM. Furthermore, as a temperature probe, the fluorescence intensity shows a linear decrease when the temperature increases from 25 °C to 100 °C, and reversible and stable abilities of temperature sensing are demonstrated. Finally, the potential of our synthesized N-GQDs in optoelectronics is demonstrated. Our study may pave the way for exploring N-GQDs as multifunctional sensors and optoelectronic devices, especially in TC detection, temperature sensing, and white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs).

Benzimidazoles, anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), tetracycline and other reagents were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and were of analytical grade without any further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments was prepared by the Millipore system with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.

Morphologies of the as-synthesized N-GQDs were revealed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) on Hitachi H-8100, and Atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were utilized to investigate surface chemical compositions and functional groups of N-GQDs. The fluorescence spectra were acquired by PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.), and the UV–vis spectrophotometer was introduced for measuring the absorption spectrum of N-GQDs. The Raman spectra (SPLCX40 M, Raman) were carried out using an Ar+ laser with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm.

0.23 g accurately weighed benzimidazole was dispersed into 20 mL absolute ethanol with lightly stirring, and the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, then heated for 6 h at 180 °C. After cooling down to the room temperature, the products were filtered by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (100−500 Da MWCO) for 3 days. Finally the obtained solution was freeze-dried and stored in a brown reagent bottle for further use.

Quinine sulfate (0.01 M H2SO4; relative QY∼0.54) was selected as a reference. The QY of N-GQDs were calculated according to the Equation [24],
(1)
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where φ is the QY of N-GQDs, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A stands for the optical density, and the subscript R refers to the reference standard with a known quantum yield. In order to keep the reliability of experimental data, the samples should meet the absorption under 0.1 at 360 nm excitation wavelength [25].

Prior to the detection of TC, 1 mmol/L tetracycline standard solution was diluted to different concentrations with ultrapure water during the course of the experiment. N-GQDs were dispersed into 10 mM phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer solution at pH 6.5−7.5. Then, 10 μL of TC solutions with different concentrations were added to 2 mL of the obtained N-GQDs solution (1 mg/mL) at room temperature (25 ± 0.5 °C). The resultant solutions were incubated for 1 min at room temperature under well-mixing conditions. After incubation at the specific time intervals, the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded at the wavelength of 357 nm.

The effect of temperature on fluorescence was surveyed. A water cycling system with tunable temperature was employed to guarantee the constant temperature condition, and the fluorescence spectra were measured under the optimum excitation wavelength (357 nm).

The epoxy resin was chosen to construct the LED luminous film, epoxy resin is a familiar thermoset resin and extensive apply in paints, binder and sealant for electronic device, especially the encapsulation of the LED chips. Then according to the principle of three primary colors, the high-quality WLEDs were fabricated by using commercial yellow phosphors and the as-prepared N-GQDs. The two materials were dissolved in the epoxy resin solution and then adding the hardener with 1:3 vol ratio to form a clear solution. Finally, the solution was dried at room temperature to obtain the transparent films and the WLEDs were made by the films covered on the LED chips (365 nm).

Fig. 1
(a) shows the TEM result of the as-synthesized N-GQDs. It is clearly shown that N-GQDs are mainly spherical and have a good dispersion with the average particle size of about 3.8 nm (according to the statistical analysis of more than 100 dots in the inset of Fig. 1 (a)). A typical high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) reveals the lattice spacing is 0.24 nm in Fig. 1 (a), which corresponds to the (100) lattice fringe of graphene [26]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis pattern in Fig. S1 demonstrated a standard six-fold symmetry pattern, indicating a high crystalline structure of the N-GQDs. The AFM image of N-GQDs is displayed in Fig. 1 (b), where the mean of thickness is about 0.76 nm, suggesting 1–2 layers of graphene [27]. Fig. 1 (c) shows the Raman spectrum of the prepared N-GQDs, where three primary peaking appeared. The peaks located around 1350, 1580 and 2700 cm−1 can be associated with the D band, G band and 2D band in graphene structure, respectively. The ID/IG is calculated as about 0.41 and the appearance of 2D band indicates the high quality of the prepared N-GQDs, similar to that of few-layered graphene nanosheets [28]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N-GQDs in Fig. 1 (d) exhibits a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 24.7°, which designate the existence of graphitic lattice in the synthesized N-GQDs structure. Compared to the typical peak (002) at 26.5°, the shift of the as synthesized N-GQDs could be explained as the decline in ordered sp2 layers rely on high defective carbon structures on the N-GQDs [29].

The chemical compositions of N-GQDs are investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As highlighted in Fig. 2
(a), the prepared N-GQDs exhibits three characteristic bind energy signals of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s at 284.8, 400.1 and 531.1 eV, and the calculated atomic percentages are 55.47, 16.29 and 28.24 %, respectively. The existence of the oxygen element may be attributed to ethanol introduced in the carbonization reaction. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s could be deconvoluted into four peaks locating at 284.3, 285.2, 286.8 and 288 eV, as shown in Fig. 2 (b), which represent CC/C = C, CO, C = CN and CO/C = N, respectively [30]. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2 (c)) demonstrates two peaks of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, which located at 399.4 and 401.2 eV [31]. Fig. 2 (d) shows two O-related bonding fitted peaks at 530.6 and 532.0 eV, attributing to the CO and OCO/COH bonds, respectively [32]. To further characterized the surface status of N-GQDs, the FT-IR spectrum is collected. As shown in Fig. 2 (e), the broadband peaks at 2800−3200 cm−1 are corresponds to −OH and NH2 groups. The stretching vibrations of CO/CN and CC are situated at 1655 and 1530 cm−1, respectively [30,33]. Furthermore, the intense absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 is attribute to the CN stretching vibration. And the characteristic peak at 1036 cm−1 could be ascribed as the OCO bond [33]. Above observations suggest that the prepared N-GQDs have been modified by the rich functional groups with nitrogen and oxygen elements on the surface, resulting in good water-solubility and excellent fluorescence performance. According to the analysis results, the chemical structure of N-GQDs is schematically speculated in Fig. 2 (f).

PL spectra and UV–vis absorption are mighty tools to investigate the optical properties of N-GQDs. The UV–vis absorption in Fig. 3
(a) shows two typical absorption peaks at 248 and 280 nm, standing for the π−π* transition of CC and n−π* transition of CO or CN [34]. Besides, the optical excitation wavelength was 357 nm and the optical emission wavelength was 435 nm. The N-GQDs are nearly colorless under sunlight, but it shows strong blue emission under a 365 nm UV light. The QY of the as-prepared N-GQDs is 25 % by utilizing an integrated sphere under the excitation of 360 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 (b), it is found that the emission wavelength of the as-synthesized N-GQDs is strongly depended on the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength increases gradually from 315 to 405 nm (the step length is 10 nm), the emission wavelength shows a distinct red shift from 372 to 464 nm. This excitation-dependence of the fluorescence emission spectrum indicates the existence of the surface emissive trap and red-edge effect of N-GQDs [35,36]. Fig. 3 (c) expresses the two-dimensional mapping of emission (λem) and excitation (λex) of N-GQDs, with a strong contrast and non-circular area (around λem = 435 nm, λex = 357 nm), intuitively reflecting the dependence of emission and excitation wavelength. Moreover, the fluorescent stabilities of N-GQDs under different environments are also studied. Fig. 3 (d) reveals the PL intensity of N-GQDs under various pH values. Although the PL intensity can be affected by pH, the fluorescence intensity still maintains 80 % at pH 13. Besides, the fluorescence intensity only decreases by about 6.8 % under ultraviolet exposure (365 nm) for 18 h, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 (e), and maintains 90 % after storing at room temperature up to 10 days, as shown in Fig. 3 (f). The above analysis results indicate that the synthesized N-GQDs possess good optical performance and outstanding stability, suggesting the immense potential in various practical situations.

Prior to exploring the TC detection capability of N-GQDs, the reaction time is firstly optimized. The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs is acutely quenched within 1 min upon the addition of TC, and then keeps stable, as shown in Fig. S2, suggesting the sensitive, rapid detection ability of N-GQDs towards TC in real time. As shown in Fig. 4
(a), we explore the fluorescence intensity ration (F/F0) of the N-GQDs with different antibiotics and amino acids (the concentrations are 100 μM) at 357 nm excitation wavelength, and all tests are performed at room temperature. The results demonstrate that the TC can cause obvious fluorescence quenching compared with other antibiotics. In order to further prove the selectivity and anti-interference of the N-GQDs, the effect of various metal ions on the PL intensity of the N-GQDs is studied in Fig. S3. The results show that the PL intensity is not significant difference, suggesting outstanding selectivity and eximious anti-interference to TC of the as-synthesized N-GQDs. Fig. 4 (b) visually displays the digital images of N-GQDs solution with adding various concentrations of TC under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. The fluorescence was drastically reduced due to the presence of TC, and when the concentration of TC reaches to 300 μM, the N-GQDs solution is almost no fluorescence. Then a quantitative detection of TC was performed under the optimal reaction time. As shown in Fig. 4 (c), the PL intensity of N-GQDs was gradually decreased with the increased concentration of TC from 0 to 400 μM. Fig. 4 (d) showed the relationship between (F0-F)/F and the concentration of TC. Noteworthy, a good linearity (the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996) at the low concentration range from 0 to 60 μM is found, with a detection limit of 0.21 μM. Table S1 shows the ability of various materials from reported literatures for detecting TC. The results suggest the prepared N-GQDs are superior fluorescence probes and have great prospects for TC detection [37–43].

The mechanism of highly effective detection of TC is investigated. As shown in Fig. 5
(a), there is no newly appeared absorbance peak before and after the addition of TC, indicating that no complexes have been formed between N-GQDs and TC. The Raman spectra and the FT-IR spectra are shown in Figs. S4 and S5, respectively. The changes of the Raman spectra can be ignored, in other words, the crystalline structure of the N-GQDs is not changed after adding TC. Besides, the absorption peaks of the FT-IR spectra have no obviously shift as well as the new absorption site, suggesting that no functional groups are formed or combined on the surface of the N-GQDs [30]. To seek the specific mechanism of the TC detection, the UV absorption spectra of TC is investigated. The absorption band of TC has a large overlap area with the excitation band of N-GQDs in Fig. 5 (b). Moreover, Fig. S6 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of different antibiotics, amino acids and metal ions. Only Ni2+ ions and cefazolin have a small overlap with the excitation spectrum of N-GQDs. In order to evaluate the interference, various concentrations of Ni2+ ions or cefazolin are added, and the corresponding PL intensity of the N-GQDs is studied. As shown in Fig. S7, no significant difference of the PL intensity is observed, suggesting that the mechanism of TC detection may be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or internal filter effect (IFE) [44]. Nevertheless, the average fluorescence life of N-GQDs (τ1 = 4.18 ns) is similar to the lifetime of N-GQDs after adding TC (τ2 = 4.23 ns) in Fig. 5 (c), indicating no FRET process occurs between N-GQDs and TC [36,45]. Therefore, the highly efficient IFE is the main contribution for the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs in the detection of TC.

Meanwhile, the obtained N-GQDs also demonstrate obviously temperature-dependent fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 6
(a), when the temperature changes from 25 to 100 °C, the fluorescence intensity is continuously decreases. Images of the N-GQDs dispersion heated at the temperature from 10 and 80 °C under ultraviolet exposure (365 nm) light are shown in Fig. 6 (b). Obviously, the N-GQDs express intense fluorescence at 10 °C, while matted emission was observed at 80 °C. The continuous variations of the fluorescence images are shown in Fig. S8. Furthermore, according to the Fig. 6 (c), as the temperature increased, the red line represents the good linear dependence between I/I0 values and temperature, and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.994. Inversely, when the temperature decreases, the changes of I/I0 values also show a good linear relationship with the R2 = 0.997. Fig. 6 (d) demonstrates the stability and repeatability of N-GQDs by heating and cooling experiments. It shows that the fluorescence intensity tapers off as the temperature goes from 25 to 100 °C, then recovers to its initial value when temperature comes back to 25 °C. Moreover, the process can be repeated many times and behaves almost no fluorescence loss, which means robust, stable and wide-range sensing ability of N-GQDs for temperature sensing. As per the previous reports [46–48], the temperature-dependent fluorescence of N-GQDs can be ascribed to the photo-excited electron such as radiative relaxation, non-radiative relaxation and thermally activated trap on surface and impurity states. As the temperature raises, more electrons return from the excited state to the ground state, thus weakening the fluorescence intensity. On the contrary, as the temperature decreases, the electrons return to the excited state to restore the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the N-GQDs are suitable for serving as a luminescence thermometry material with high sensitivity and repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-Quality white LED assembled by the N-GQDs phosphors
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the intense blue fluorescence, gentle preparation and slightly toxicity, the prepared N-GQDs are further utilized to construct WLEDs. Based on the principle of three primary colors, a high-quality white LED is fabricated by using commercial yellow phosphors and the as-prepared N-GQDs. The WLEDs are constructed by the fluorescence film covered on the LED ultraviolet chip (365 nm). It is found that the constructed WLEDs display high-quality white light at the voltage of 3.0 V, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra is shown in Fig. 7
(a). The peak position around 365 nm belongs to the LED UV chip, and the emission peaks located at 435 and 555 nm are attributed to the N-GQDs phosphors and the commercial yellow phosphors, respectively. The fluorescence spectra covers the entire visible light range, and the CIE coordinates is (0.357, 0.315) with the color temperature is 4427 K. The color point was marked on the black body Planckian locus in Fig. 7 (b).

In summary, multifunctional N-GQDs have been successfully synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment with benzimidazole as the precursor. The as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited uniform size, good crystalline properties, and bright fluorescence intensity at room temperature. Noteworthy, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs is strongly depended on TC with a superior selectivity. Therefore, a label-free and sensitive fluorescence probe for TC, with the detection limit of 0.21 μM, is demonstrated. Moreover, the PL intensity of N-GQDs also express temperature dependence in a wide range (25−100 °C), thus the N-GQDs are further developed as a luminescent temperature measuring material. Besides, the possibility of N-GQDs as a phosphor in fabricating WLEDs are investigated and proved, with the CIE coordinates (0.357, 0.315) and color temperature of 4427 K. Our work may provide material strategies and device architectures for exploiting GQDs for the applications in fluorescence sensing, detecting and optoelectronic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112977.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: [details of any supplementary information available should be included here]. Figures (Figs. S1-S8). See DOI:
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of copper doped carbon dots and their application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe in the detection of Fe3+ ions
RSC Adv.

Adv. Mater

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A yellow carbon dots-based phosphor with high efficiency for white light-emitting devices
J. Lumin.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescence probe for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and glutathione
Sens. Actuators B Chem.
